title: Extremal Lagrangian Tori in Toric Domains
speaker: Shah Faisal
abstract: The symplectic area of a Lagrangian submanifold L in a symplectic manifold is defined as the minimal positive symplectic area of a smooth 2-disk with boundary on L. A Lagrangian torus is called extremal if it maximizes the symplectic area among all Lagrangian tori. I will explain that every extremal Lagrangian torus in the standard symplectic unit ball is entirely contained in the boundary of the ball. This result confirms a conjecture of Cieliebak and Mohnke in the affirmative. (Ref: arXiv:2504.13076)

proof_stages.label: Local tangency constraints and constrained curve counts
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the local tangency/contact-order condition for holomorphic spheres through a point, computes the expected dimension of the constrained moduli space, and states that rigid signed counts are invariant under the auxiliary choices.

proof_stages.label: Degree-one CP^n count and compactness of tangency constraints
proof_stages.summary: For CP^n in the line class the constrained count is (n-1)!, and a compactness lemma explains how tangency survives or is distributed in nodal limits so that rigid monotone moduli spaces remain compact generically.

proof_stages.label: Lagrangian capacity and the extremal-torus problem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker introduces toric domains, their diagonal, the symplectic area and Lagrangian capacity of a torus, formulates Cieliebak-Mohnke conjectures about extremal tori, and surveys known progress and consequences.

proof_stages.label: Neck-stretching constrained lines around an assumed interior extremal torus
proof_stages.summary: Assuming an extremal torus in the unit ball has an interior point, the ball is enlarged and compactified to CP^n, constrained degree-one spheres are stretched along a Weinstein neighborhood of the torus, and the resulting building is analyzed as a bottom punctured sphere with n+1 top planes.

proof_stages.label: Area estimate and conditional monotonicity contradiction
proof_stages.summary: Compactifying the top planes gives disks whose areas force the component meeting the hyperplane at infinity to have arbitrarily small area, which would contradict monotonicity if it could be deformed to pass through a fixed interior point.

proof_stages.label: Borman-Sheridan class and gluing a disk through the interior point
proof_stages.summary: Positive symplectic cohomology of the cotangent bundle and the Borman-Sheridan class identify a top plane with nonzero count, and gluing it to a cotangent-bundle cylinder through the chosen point supplies the disk needed for the monotonicity contradiction.

proof_stages.label: Toric-domain applications and limits of boundary containment
proof_stages.summary: The boundary-containment method is applied to cylinders and four-dimensional toric domains enclosed by ellipsoids of the same diagonal, while a nonconvex union-of-cylinders example shows that the naive boundary-containment conjecture fails in general.

proof_stages.label: Four-dimensional constrained-plane strategy
proof_stages.summary: For the four-dimensional toric-domain theorem, the speaker replaces compactification by counts of constrained holomorphic planes asymptotic to ellipsoid Reeb orbits, uses automatic transversality for a rigid cylinder, and quotes a nonvanishing theorem for such plane counts.

examples.name: Non-disjoint union of cylinders with Hopf lifts of area-halving loops
examples.note: This counterexample shows that an extremal torus can meet the interior in a nonconvex toric domain, forcing the boundary-containment statement to be restricted to suitable convex or round settings.
examples.engagement_reason: he constructs Hopf lifts of loops in CP^1 bounding half the area inside the non-disjoint union of cylinders and explains how non-equatorial loops give extremal tori meeting the interior

passes.reason: A non-equatorial area-halving loop has a Hopf-lift extremal torus in the cylinder union that enters the interior, disproving the boundary-containment conjecture there.

examples.name: Vianna tori lifted to the six-ball
examples.note: Known infinitely many monotone Vianna tori in CP^2 illustrate that the higher-dimensional ball has infinitely many Hamiltonian-isotopy classes of extremal tori.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies the boundary reduction/lift correspondence to Vianna monotone tori in CP^2 to produce infinitely many Hamiltonian isotopy classes of extremal tori in the six-ball

passes.reason: Lifts of Vianna monotone tori give extremal six-ball tori in infinitely many Hamiltonian isotopy classes, refuting higher-dimensional uniqueness.
